results.title: Gas issuance is inflated and will halt the chain or lead to incorrect base fee
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit item #15 identifies the same root cause in TaikoL2’s EIP-1559 basefee logic: issuance is computed as (_l1BlockId - lastSyncedBlock) * gasTarget while lastSyncedBlock is only updated every BLOCK_SYNC_THRESHOLD blocks, causing triangular over-subtraction of gas excess. It cites the same TaikoL2 code path (_calc1559BaseFee / lastSyncedBlock handling) and explains the same consequence (broken fee market; basefee mismatch/deflation). Fixing the issuance/lastSyncedBlock logic as per the report would also fix the described vulnerability.

results.title: Validity and contests bond ca be incorrectly burned for the correct and ultimately verified transition
results.reasoning: The described issue is a logic/economic flaw where, after higher-tier/guardian overrides, prior provers/contesters can have their validity and contest bonds effectively burned or locked because _overrideWithHigherProof resets ts fields and guardians can only refund liveness bonds. None of the audit items identify this specific problem. The closest items (#39, #78) discuss liveness bond penalties when a higher-tier prover overrides, and #37/#68 concern guardian-controlled liveness bond refunds, but none mention wrongful slashing or lack of refund for validity/contest bonds, nor the reset of ts.validityBond/ts.contestBond or funds being stuck with the guardian. Fixes proposed in the report would not fix the vulnerability in the description, and vice versa, so the report did not detect this specific issue.

results.title: Users will never be able to withdraw their claimed airdrop fully in ERC20Airdrop2.sol contract
results.reasoning: The report lists several ERC20Airdrop2 issues (e.g., anyone can call withdraw, division-by-zero if withdrawalWindow=0, underflow when extending claimEnd) but does not identify the specific flaw where users cannot fully withdraw due to the ongoingWithdrawals time window preventing withdrawals after claimEnd + withdrawalWindow, making full withdrawal practically impossible. None of the reported findings fix or mention the time-boundary logic in ongoingWithdrawals. Therefore, the specific vulnerability was not detected.

results.title: Taiko L1 - Proposer can maliciously cause loss of funds by forcing someone else to pay prover’s fee
results.reasoning: Yes. The audit’s Issue 40 (“Arbitrary ERC20 Theft via Unvalidated coinbase in AssignmentHook”) describes the same flaw and code path: LibProposing allows an arbitrary params.coinbase, which is passed to AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed where safeTransferFrom(_meta.coinbase, assignedProver, fee) pulls ERC20 from that address. This enables a proposer to force another address (with allowance) to pay the prover’s fee. Fixing by enforcing coinbase == msg.sender (or equivalent) addresses both descriptions. Same vulnerability mechanism, same functions, same fix.

results.title: Signatures can be replayed in withdraw() to withdraw more tokens than the user originally intended.
results.reasoning: Yes. The report’s Issue 7 analyzes TimelockTokenPool.withdraw(address,bytes) using a signature over keccak256("Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ", _to) without chainId/contract/nonce. It explicitly notes the absence of a nonce. Although the report focuses on cross-chain/cross-instance replay, the underlying flaw is the same missing replay protection in withdraw(), and the same fix (add nonce/EIP-712 domain separation) would also prevent replay on the same contract to withdraw more than intended. Thus, it detected the same vulnerability in the same function and code path with the same root cause.
